crosswalk.notes: Both evaluate constructing and solving one-variable linear equations. SAT emphasizes context modeling, while ACT contains more straightforward algebra calculations.
Both cover systems of two linear equations. ACT occasionally introduces systems of three linear equations or matrix representation equivalents, which are not tested on the SAT.
Both evaluate basic trigonometry (Sine, Cosine, Tangent) in right triangles. ACT extends trigonometry questions to graphs of trig functions and trig identities (e.g. sin^2(x) + cos^2(x) = 1).
Clause boundary rules (semicolons, colons, dash usage, comma rules) are identical between the exams.
Both test transitions between sentences or paragraphs (addition, contrast, cause-and-effect transitions).
